notes: Cookie bar format already saturated in US (Quest bars, built bars, etc.). Hard to differentiate on format alone.
Gluten-free/vegan chocolate cornflake cookies is complex formulation. Margins would be tight competing with established GF brands.
Baking powder + recipes already done by King Arthur, Bob's Red Mill. QR codes not enough differentiation.
Yeast extract spread has tried and failed in US multiple times. Even UK Marmite barely sells. Cultural taste barrier too high.
US has dozens of premium ketchup/tomato sauce brands (Sir Kensington's, Primal Kitchen, etc.). Saturated market.
Golden syrup sold by Lyle's in US. Stick pack format not compelling enough vs. squeeze bottles already available.
Super-chunky and protein peanut butter categories already crowded (Jif Extra Crunchy, RX Nut Butter, etc.).
Keto/seed crackers is saturated category (Simple Mills, Hu Kitchen, dozens of brands on Amazon).
Protein gingerbread cookies exist (Lenny & Larry's has flavors close to this). Hard to differentiate.
Milo is already sold in US at international markets. Cold brew chocolate malt concept has been tried (Ovaltine, etc.).
Weetabix sells in US. Protein fortified wheat biscuits would compete with established protein bar market.
Paleo cookie mixes already crowded (Simple Mills, Purely Elizabeth, etc.). Anzac name has no US recognition.
Cheese powder for keto mac widely available (Hoosier Hill Farm, Anthony's, etc.).
Protein peanut butter chocolate bars saturated (Built Bar, One Bar, Barebells, etc.). Hard to stand out.
Meal kit seasoning subscriptions exist (Spice Tribe, RawSpiceBar). Hard to differentiate without unique spice blends.
Bone broth gravy concept interesting but keto gravy mixes already available (multiple brands on Amazon).
Protein custard mix is niche within niche. Formulation would be complex to get right texture.
Licorice is divisive in US market. Sugar-free/collagen licorice is too niche and Americans don't love licorice generally.
